factors.summary: The company faces significant pension and postretirement benefit funding obligations, which may adversely affect its financial condition.

evidence.text: Our pension funding obligations are significant. The amount of these obligations will depend on the performance of investments held in trust by the pension plans, interest rates for determining liabilities and actuarial experience.
evidence.subsection: We have significant pension and other postretirement benefit funding obligations, which may adversely affect our liquidity, results of operations and financial condition.

factors.summary: The company's credit card processing agreements may impose holdback requirements, which could reduce liquidity, especially with increased refund requests due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: These credit card processing companies are not currently entitled to maintain any holdbacks pursuant to these requirements. These holdback requirements can be modified at the discretion of the credit card processing companies upon the occurrence of specific events, including material adverse changes in our financial condition or the triggering of a liquidity covenant.
evidence.subsection: If our financial condition worsens, provisions in our credit card processing and other commercial agreements may adversely affect our liquidity.

factors.summary: Management is cautiously optimistic, with actions indicating confidence in recovery through cost management and liquidity preservation.

evidence.text: We remain extremely focused on taking all self-help measures available to manage our business during this unprecedented time...
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

factors.summary: Vaccination progress and government financial assistance are key catalysts supporting recovery.

evidence.text: While global vaccination efforts are underway and demand for air travel has begun to return, the continued impact of COVID-19... has resulted in a material deterioration in our revenues.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

factors.summary: The supply chain is improving, with increased production and delivery of new aircraft.

evidence.text: As of June 30, 2021, we had definitive purchase agreements for the acquisition of the following aircraft: [...] Airbus A320neo Family, Boeing 737 MAX Family, and 787 Family.
evidence.subsection: Aircraft and Engine Purchase Commitments

factors.summary: The competitive environment is intensifying with new entrants and low-cost carriers increasing their market share.

evidence.text: Low-cost carriers (including so-called ultra-low-cost carriers) have a profound impact on industry revenues... attempting to continue to increase their market share through growth and several new entrants have announced their intention to start up new ultra-low-cost carriers and, potentially, consolidation.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: Environmental regulations and compliance efforts are creating significant costs and operational constraints.

evidence.text: All such climate change-related regulatory activity and developments may adversely affect our business and financial results by requiring us to reduce our emissions, make capital investments to purchase specific types of equipment or technologies, purchase carbon offset credits, or otherwise incur additional costs related to our emissions.
evidence.subsection: We may be adversely affected by conflicts overseas or terrorist attacks; the travel industry continues to face ongoing security concerns.

factors.summary: Labor relations are a significant risk factor, with potential for strikes and union disputes affecting operations.

evidence.text: Union disputes, employee strikes and other labor-related disruptions, or our inability to otherwise maintain labor costs at competitive levels may adversely affect our operations and financial performance.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: The company is integrating technology systems, but this process carries risks and could impact operations.

evidence.text: While we have to date successfully integrated many of our computer, communication and other technology systems in connection with the merger of US Airways and American, including our customer reservations system and our pilot, flight attendant and fleet scheduling system, we still have to complete several additional important system integration or replacement projects.
evidence.subsection: We face challenges in integrating our computer, communications and other technology systems.

factors.summary: Market share is at risk due to competitive pressures and potential customer perceptions influenced by external factors.

evidence.text: Changes in consumer preferences, perceptions, spending patterns and demographic trends; changes in the competitive environment due to industry consolidation, changes in airline alliance affiliations, and other factors could affect our market share.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: Customer concentration is not explicitly addressed, but diversification of customer base is implied as a risk factor.

evidence.text: Customer perceptions of our advertising campaigns, sponsorship arrangements or marketing programs, customer perceptions of our use of social media, or customer perceptions of statements made by us, our employees and executives, agents or other third parties could affect our reputation and customer base.
evidence.subsection: Any damage to our reputation or brand image could adversely affect our business or financial results.

factors.summary: The company's competitive differentiation is at risk due to market dynamics and potential loss of customer trust.

evidence.text: Damage to our reputation or brand image or loss of customer confidence in our services could adversely affect our business and financial results, as well as require additional resources to rebuild our reputation.
evidence.subsection: Any damage to our reputation or brand image could adversely affect our business or financial results.

factors.summary: Headcount restructuring is not explicitly detailed, but labor relations and retention challenges suggest potential workforce adjustments.

evidence.text: Our business, results of operations and financial condition have been and will continue to be affected by many changing economic and other conditions beyond our control, including global events that affect travel behavior, and our results of operations could be volatile and fluctuate due to seasonality.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: The duration and severity of the COVID-19 pandemic remain uncertain, and there can be no assurance that any of the mitigating actions we have taken will suffice to sustain our business and operations through this pandemic.

factors.summary: The company is taking steps to manage its debt leverage, including debt refinancing, early adoption of convertible debt standards, and repayment of term loans, which indicate a focus on improving its financial structure. However, the company faces significant risks due to high existing indebtedness and a non-investment grade credit rating, which could affect its ability to obtain additional financing.

evidence.text: We early adopted the New Convertible Debt Standard as of January 1, 2021 using the modified retrospective method to recognize our Convertible Notes as a single liability instrument. As of January 1, 2021, we recorded a $415 million reduction to additional paid-in capital and a $19 million cumulative effect adjustment credit.
evidence.subsection: Contractual Obligations

evidence.text: Our substantial level of existing indebtedness, particularly following the additional liquidity transactions completed and contemplated in response to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic; our non-investment grade credit rating;
evidence.subsection: We will need to obtain sufficient financing or other capital to operate successfully.

evidence.text: On July 22, 2021, we repaid in full at par and terminated the term loan.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity

factors.summary: The company's capital expenditure outlook is mixed. While it is maintaining its capital expenditures as per its credit agreement terms, it is also cautious, focusing on reducing costs and improving liquidity. Additionally, the integration of technology systems suggests potential future investments, though capital expenditures are not explicitly discussed in this context.

evidence.text: We have reduced our system-wide capacity and will continue to monitor conditions and to proactively evaluate and adjust our schedule to match demand. Additionally, we have retired certain mainline aircraft earlier than planned...

evidence.text: The revolving credit facility component of the April 2016 Credit Agreement, which has a final maturity of October 2024, remains in place.

evidence.text: While we have to date successfully integrated many of our computer, communication and other technology systems in connection with the merger of US Airways and American, including our customer reservations system and our pilot, flight attendant and fleet scheduling system, we still have to complete several additional important system integration or replacement projects.
evidence.subsection: We face challenges in integrating our computer, communications and other technology systems.

factors.summary: The company faces rising fuel costs and is currently not hedging against these increases, which exposes them to significant price fluctuations. This lack of hedging could negatively impact their profitability and cash flow, especially given the potential for further increases in fuel prices.

evidence.text: An increase in costs, either due to an increase in borrowing costs caused by a reduction in credit ratings or a general increase in interest rates, or due to an increase in the cost of fuel, maintenance, aircraft, aircraft engines or parts, could decrease the amount of cash available to cover cash contractual obligations.
evidence.subsection: Capital Raising Activity and Other Possible Actions

evidence.text: As of June 30, 2021, we did not have any fuel hedging contracts outstanding to hedge our fuel consumption.
evidence.subsection: AAG’s Second Quarter 2021 Results

evidence.text: Aircraft fuel and related taxes increased $552 million, or 26.4%, in the first six months of 2021 from the first six months of 2020 due to increased capacity as well as an increase in the average price per gallon of aircraft fuel including related taxes.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2021 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2020

evidence.text: we did not have any fuel hedging contracts outstanding to hedge our fuel consumption. As such, and assuming we do not enter into any future transactions to hedge our fuel consumption, we will continue to be fully exposed to fluctuations in fuel prices.
evidence.subsection: The commercial relationships that we have with other airlines, including any related equity investment, may not produce the returns or results we expect.

factors.summary: The company faces a range of near-term risks, including the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on demand and operational costs, rising fuel expenses, challenges in managing increased demand, potential economic downturns, and risks related to third-party distribution channels, such as higher costs and limited functionality. Additionally, there are concerns about pilot shortages, increased insurance costs, high taxes and fees, and the volatility of the company's stock price.

evidence.text: The duration and severity of the COVID-19 pandemic could result in additional adverse effects on our business, operating results, financial condition and liquidity. The full extent of the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on our longer-term operational and financial performance will depend on future developments, many of which are outside our control.
evidence.subsection: Risks Related to our Business

evidence.text: Aircraft fuel and related taxes increased $1.3 billion in the second quarter of 2021 from the second quarter of 2020 due to increased capacity as well as a 69.5% increase in the average price per gallon of aircraft fuel including related taxes to $1.91 in the second quarter of 2021 from $1.13 in the second quarter of 2020.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2021 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2020

evidence.text: We rely on third-party distribution channels... to distribute a significant portion of our airline tickets... These distribution channels are more expensive and at present have less functionality in respect of ancillary product offerings than those we operate ourselves... To remain competitive, we will need to manage successfully our distribution costs and rights, increase our distribution flexibility and improve the functionality of our distribution channels, while maintaining an industry-competitive cost structure.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

evidence.text: A higher than normal number of pilot retirements, more stringent duty time regulations... have caused a shortage of pilots that could materially adversely affect our business.
evidence.subsection: We rely on third-party distribution channels and must manage effectively the costs, rights and functionality of these channels.

evidence.text: The terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001 led to a significant increase in insurance premiums... If we are unable to maintain adequate insurance coverage, our business could be materially and adversely affected.
evidence.subsection: We rely on third-party distribution channels and must manage effectively the costs, rights and functionality of these channels.

evidence.text: The airline industry is subject to extensive government fees and taxation that negatively impact our revenue and profitability.
evidence.subsection: We rely on third-party distribution channels and must manage effectively the costs, rights and functionality of these channels.

evidence.text: The market price of AAG common stock has fluctuated substantially in the past, and may fluctuate substantially in the future, due to a variety of factors, many of which are beyond our control.
evidence.subsection: Interruptions or disruptions in service at one of our key facilities could have a material adverse impact on our operations.

factors.summary: The company's shareholder returns are currently constrained due to financial and regulatory limitations, which prohibit share repurchases and dividend payments until September 2022. However, the company is generating strong operating cash flows and has financing activities in place to maintain financial stability, which are key factors influencing future shareholder returns.

evidence.text: In connection with the financial assistance we have received through PSP1, PSP2 and PSP3, we are required to comply with the relevant provisions...prohibiting the repurchase of AAG common stock and the payment of common stock dividends through September 30, 2022.

evidence.text: Our net cash provided by operating activities was $3.6 billion for the first six months of 2021 as compared to net cash used in operating activities of $1.1 billion for the first six months of 2020, a $4.7 billion period-over-period increase.

factors.summary: The margin trajectory appears to be mixed, with some evidence indicating improvement due to cost reductions and higher revenues, while other sections suggest ongoing pressure from rising costs and operational challenges.

evidence.text: Gross and operating margins are under pressure due to increased costs and reduced demand.

factors.summary: The company has implemented cost reduction initiatives, which have delivered measurable savings, particularly through fleet impairments and early retirements, contributing to margin improvements. evidence.text: We have taken and will take additional actions to improve our financial position, including measures to improve liquidity, such as obtaining financial assistance under the CARES Act, the PSP Extension Law and the ARP.

evidence.text: Fleet impairment resulted from our decision to retire certain aircraft earlier than planned driven primarily by the severe decline in air travel due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

factors.summary: Demand for air travel is showing signs of recovery and acceleration, particularly in leisure travel and domestic markets. However, the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic continues to pose challenges, with reduced demand in certain regions and the expectation of continued disruptions.

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $936 million, or 10.7%, in the first six months of 2021 from the first six months of 2020 primarily due to an increase in RPMs. This increase in RPMs was principally due to increased flying domestically and in Latin America driven by leisure demand, offset in part by reduced Atlantic and Pacific flying as a result of low passenger demand and government-imposed travel restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $936 million, or 10.7%, in the first six months of 2021 from the first six months of 2020 primarily due to an increase in RPMs. Cargo revenue increased $364 million in the first six months of 2021 from the first six months of 2020 primarily due to an increase in cargo ton miles reflecting higher freight volumes and the addition of cargo-only flights to American's schedule as well as an increase in cargo yield as a result of higher rates.

factors.summary: The company's near-term results are influenced by several key sensitivities, including fuel prices, which have shown significant increases, and interest rates. Additionally, labor costs and environmental regulations, such as those related to CORSIA, pose risks. The impact of fuel prices is evident in both cost increases and cost efficiency improvements, while regulatory changes and economic conditions could further affect operations.

evidence.text: Aircraft fuel and related taxes increased $1.3 billion in the second quarter of 2021 from the second quarter of 2020 due to increased capacity as well as a 69.5% increase in the average price per gallon of aircraft fuel including related taxes to $1.91 in the second quarter of 2021 from $1.13 in the second quarter of 2020.

evidence.text: Our 2021 second quarter total operating cost per available seat mile (CASM) was 12.90 cents, a decrease of 46.4%, from 24.05 cents in the second quarter of 2020. evidence.text: The costs of complying with our future obligations under CORSIA are uncertain, primarily because it is difficult to estimate the return of demand for international air travel during and in the recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic. factors.summary: The company is currently raising prices due to increased demand, as evidenced by a significant increase in TRASM. However, there are risks associated with price hikes, including potential customer resistance and a possible decrease in demand, which could negatively impact revenue growth.

evidence.text: Total revenue per available seat mile (TRASM) was 13.71 cents in the second quarter of 2021, a 44.3% increase as compared to 9.50 cents in the second quarter of 2020.

evidence.text: the implementation of any new or increased fees might reduce the demand for air travel on our airline or across the industry in general...

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is showing signs of recovery, particularly in domestic markets, but there are ongoing adjustments due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. While domestic demand is recovering, international capacity remains significantly reduced, and the company expects to maintain reduced regional capacity levels in the near term.

evidence.text: Domestic capacity in the second quarter of 2021 was down 12.8% while international capacity was down 46.5% versus 2019, but demand is recovering in domestic markets.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

evidence.text: we have significantly reduced our regional capacity and expect to maintain these reduced levels of capacity for the foreseeable future.

factors.summary: The company is shifting its revenue mix in multiple areas, including a favorable shift towards leisure demand domestically and in Latin America, an increase in cargo revenue due to higher freight volumes and yields, and the introduction of new services and fee structures. However, these changes may pose risks to demand and margins.

evidence.text: The quarter-over-quarter improvement in our pre-tax income (loss), on both a GAAP basis and excluding pre-tax net special items, was primarily due to higher revenues driven by strong leisure demand domestically and in Latin America.
evidence.subsection: AAG’s Second Quarter 2021 Results

evidence.text: Cargo revenue increased $364 million in the first six months of 2021 from the first six months of 2020 primarily due to an increase in cargo ton miles reflecting higher freight volumes and the addition of cargo-only flights to American's schedule as well as an increase in cargo yield as a result of higher rates.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2021 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2020

evidence.text: We have in the past instituted, and intend to institute in the future, changes to our business model designed to increase revenues and offset costs. These measures include... charging separately for services that had previously been included within the price of a ticket, changing (whether it be increasing, decreasing or eliminating) other pre-existing fees...
evidence.subsection: Changes to our business model that are designed to increase revenues may not be successful and may cause operational difficulties or decreased demand.

factors.summary: Free cash flow generation has shown improvement due to increased operating cash flows, but it is also negatively impacted by ongoing operational challenges and COVID-19 related costs, which are expected to continue.

evidence.text: Our net cash provided by operating activities was $3.6 billion for the first six months of 2021 as compared to net cash used in operating activities of $1.1 billion for the first six months of 2020, a $4.7 billion period-over-period increase.
evidence.subsection: Sources and Uses of Cash

evidence.text: we expect that we will continue to incur COVID-19 related costs as we sanitize aircraft, implement additional hygiene-related protocols and take other actions to limit the threat of infection among our employees and passengers.

factors.summary: The company is adjusting its route network to focus on profitable domestic and short-haul international routes, as well as leisure routes, while reducing capacity in weaker markets and suspending long-haul international flights. These adjustments are expected to reduce capacity and potentially impact profitability, particularly in the face of low passenger demand and government-imposed travel restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: We will continue to match our forward capacity with observed booking trends for future travel and make further adjustments to our capacity as needed.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $936 million, or 10.7%, in the first six months of 2021 from the first six months of 2020 primarily due to an increase in RPMs. This increase in RPMs was principally due to increased flying domestically and in Latin America driven by leisure demand, offset in part by reduced Atlantic and Pacific flying as a result of low passenger demand and government-imposed travel restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2021 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2020

factors.summary: Regulatory changes are impacting the company in multiple areas, including increased compliance costs related to climate change and restrictions on executive compensation.

evidence.text: Efforts to transition to a low-carbon future have increased the focus by global, regional and national regulators on climate change and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, including carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions.
evidence.subsection: We may be adversely affected by conflicts overseas or terrorist attacks; the travel industry continues to face ongoing security concerns.

evidence.text: the CARES Act, the PSP Extension Law and the ARP impose significant restrictions on executive compensation, which will remain in place until April 1, 2023.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: The company faces significant environmental compliance costs due to regulations like CORSIA and GHG emission standards, and maintaining high environmental sustainability practices is critical to preserving its reputation. However, failure to adhere to these standards could harm its brand image and financial results.

evidence.text: In particular, the International Civil Aviation Organization has adopted rules, including those pertaining to the Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation (CORSIA), which will require American to address the growth in CO2 emissions of a significant majority of our international flights.
evidence.subsection: We may be adversely affected by conflicts overseas or terrorist attacks; the travel industry continues to face ongoing security concerns.

evidence.text: Maintaining a good reputation globally is critical to our business. Our reputation or brand image could be adversely impacted by, among other things, any failure to maintain high ethical, social and environmental sustainability practices for all of our operations and activities...
evidence.subsection: Any damage to our reputation or brand image could adversely affect our business or financial results.

factors.summary: The company is currently not engaged in significant merger and acquisition activity. However, past integrations, particularly related to the merger of US Airways and American, indicate potential future M&A activity, which could pose integration challenges.

evidence.text: There have been no material changes in our off-balance sheet arrangements as discussed in our 2020 Form 10-K.
evidence.subsection: Off-Balance Sheet Arrangements

evidence.text: While we have to date successfully integrated many of our computer, communication and other technology systems in connection with the merger of US Airways and American, including our customer reservations system and our pilot, flight attendant and fleet scheduling system, we still have to complete several additional important system integration or replacement projects.
evidence.subsection: We face challenges in integrating our computer, communications and other technology systems.

factors.summary: The company faces significant challenges in retaining key personnel, particularly executives, due to ongoing restrictions on executive compensation imposed by the CARES Act, PSP Extension Law, and ARP. These restrictions are expected to result in lower compensation compared to other industries, increasing retention difficulties as executives are presented with alternative opportunities outside the airline sector.

evidence.text: the CARES Act, the PSP Extension Law and the ARP impose significant restrictions on executive compensation, which will remain in place until April 1, 2023.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Such restrictions, over time, will likely result in lower executive compensation in the airline industry than is prevailing in other industries, creating increasing retention challenges in the case of executives presented with alternative, non-airline opportunities.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.
